---
title: "Controlling for Confounders"
subtitle: "The Frisch-Waugh-Lovell Theorem"
author: "Tom Hackenberg · Block 2"
date: "13 April 2025"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    embed-resources: true
    code-line-numbers: false
    highlight-style: github
    fig-align: center
execute:
  eval: true
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.api as sm
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold

rng = np.random.default_rng(99)
n   = 500
W   = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
T   = 2.0 * W + rng.normal(0, 1, n)
tau_true = 1.5
Y   = tau_true * T + 3.0 * W + rng.normal(0, 1, n)
data = pd.DataFrame({"W": W, "T": T, "Y": Y})

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.facecolor":  "white",
    "axes.facecolor":    "#f8fafc",
    "axes.spines.top":   False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "font.size": 12,
})
```

# Controlling for Confounders {background-color="#1a2744"}

### The Frisch-Waugh-Lovell Theorem

*What does "controlling for X" actually mean?*

`Causal Inference · MSc · Week 11 of 15 · 120 min`

---

## Roadmap — the lecture {.smaller}

| Part | Topic | Time |
|---|---|---|
| **1 — Geometry** | What regression does in space | ~25 min |
| **2 — Theorem** | FWL: proof + numerical verification | ~20 min |
| **3 — Failure modes** | When OLS FWL breaks | ~20 min |
| **4 — Double ML** | Replace OLS with ML | ~20 min |
| **5 — Neyman** | Why the "double" matters | ~10 min |
| **Checkpoints** | Three exam-style questions | woven in |

::: {.notes}
Welcome. Today is the algebraic engine of the entire block — Weeks 12 and 15 both build directly on what we do here, so investing in understanding FWL deeply pays compound interest later.

The goal for this lecture is unusually precise: by the end, students should be able to explain what "controlling for X" means without using the words "holding constant." That phrase is a description, not an explanation. FWL gives the actual explanation.

Pacing note: Part 1 (geometry) is the hardest section conceptually, even though it has no formulas. Slow down there. The theorem in Part 2 will land much more easily if the geometry has clicked first.
:::

---

## Roadmap — your payoff {.smaller}

**Three audiences, three payoffs:**

🎓 **Social science** — what "controlling for" *means* conceptually, not just how to do it. Parts 1–2 are your core.

💻 **CS / ML** — the algorithm and precisely where it breaks. Parts 3–4 and the cross-fitting diagram.

📈 **Econ / management** — when to trust an estimate and when to be suspicious. Parts 2–3 and the comparison table.

::: {.callout-note}
The thread across weeks: **FWL** → **Double ML** → **CATE** (Weeks 11–12–15). Today is the algebraic engine. Everything from here builds on this.
:::


# Part 1 · Geometry {background-color="#1a2744"}

*Before any formula — what is regression actually doing?*


---

## The number that should make you nervous 

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%" .smaller }
You run OLS of sales on ad spend: $\hat\beta = 3.2$

£1,000 more in ad spend → £3,200 more sales.

**The problem:** firms with better products advertise more *and* sell more. Quality drives both — it confounds the comparison.

:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%;max-width:300px;margin:auto;display:block;margin-top:10px">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrRed0" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="7" refX="8" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 9 3.5, 0 7" fill="#dc2626"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrGreen0" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="7" refX="8" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 9 3.5, 0 7" fill="#16a34a"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <circle cx="160" cy="44" r="38" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="160" y="39" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#991b1b">Product</text>
  <text x="160" y="54" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#991b1b">Quality</text>
  <circle cx="55" cy="180" r="38" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="55" y="175" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b">Ad</text>
  <text x="55" y="190" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#1e293b">Spend</text>
  <circle cx="265" cy="180" r="38" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="265" y="185" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#1e293b">Sales</text>
  <line x1="137" y1="74" x2="83" y2="148" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arrRed0)"/>
  <line x1="183" y1="74" x2="237" y2="148" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arrRed0)"/>
  <line x1="95" y1="180" x2="223" y2="180" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="2.2" marker-end="url(#arrGreen0)"/>
  <text x="160" y="173" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#15803d" font-style="italic">β = ?</text>
  <text x="82" y="118" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626" font-style="italic">backdoor</text>
  <text x="208" y="118" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626" font-style="italic">backdoor</text>
</svg>
```
:::
::::

::: {.callout-important .smaller}
**The question for today:** when you add quality as a control, what does OLS *actually do* — and why does that fix the bias?
:::

---

Think of $Y$, $T$, $X$ as **vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$** — one coordinate per observation. Regression is **projection**.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 760 320" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:96%;max-width:760px;margin:auto;display:block">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrT" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="7" refX="9" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 10 3.5, 0 7" fill="#dc2626"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrThat" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="7" refX="9" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 10 3.5, 0 7" fill="#9ca3af"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrTtilde" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="7" refX="9" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 10 3.5, 0 7" fill="#16a34a"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <rect x="0" y="255" width="760" height="42" fill="#dbeafe" rx="3"/>
  <line x1="30" y1="273" x2="720" y2="273" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2.2"/>
  <text x="728" y="278" font-size="15" fill="#1d4ed8" font-style="italic" font-weight="bold">col(X)</text>
  <text x="260" y="290" font-size="12" fill="#3b82f6" font-style="italic">(subspace spanned by your controls)</text>
  <circle cx="100" cy="273" r="5" fill="#1e293b"/>
  <line x1="100" y1="273" x2="472" y2="75" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#arrT)"/>
  <text x="484" y="70" font-size="18" font-weight="bold" fill="#dc2626">T</text>
  <text x="484" y="90" font-size="12" fill="#dc2626">raw treatment</text>
  <line x1="100" y1="273" x2="466" y2="273" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="7,4" marker-end="url(#arrThat)"/>
  <text x="240" y="250" font-size="12" fill="#6b7280" font-style="italic">T̂ = X(X′X)⁻¹X′T  (the part of T that X explains)</text>
  <line x1="478" y1="264" x2="478" y2="85" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#arrTtilde)"/>
  <text x="492" y="185" font-size="17" font-weight="bold" fill="#15803d">T̃</text>
  <text x="492" y="205" font-size="13" fill="#15803d">⊥ col(X)</text>
  <text x="492" y="222" font-size="11" fill="#15803d" font-style="italic">(what X cannot explain)</text>
  <polyline points="460,273 460,255 478,255" fill="none" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="1.8"/>
</svg>
```

::: {.callout-tip}
**T̃ is not "T with X removed."** It is the variation in T that X **cannot predict**. T̃ is orthogonal to col(X) by construction — that is what residualising does.
:::


---

Do the same for $Y$. Both residuals $\tilde T$ and $\tilde Y$ land in the space $X$ knows nothing about.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 760 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:96%;max-width:760px;margin:auto;display:block">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrT2" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="7" refX="9" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 10 3.5, 0 7" fill="#dc2626"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrTtilde2" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="7" refX="9" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 10 3.5, 0 7" fill="#16a34a"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrY" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="7" refX="9" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 10 3.5, 0 7" fill="#d97706"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrYtilde" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="7" refX="9" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 10 3.5, 0 7" fill="#92400e"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <rect x="0" y="28" width="760" height="210" fill="#f0fdf4" rx="3" opacity="0.55"/>
  <text x="22" y="48" font-size="12" fill="#15803d" font-style="italic">← space orthogonal to col(X) — T̃ and Ỹ both live here →</text>
  <rect x="0" y="242" width="760" height="42" fill="#dbeafe" rx="3"/>
  <line x1="30" y1="260" x2="720" y2="260" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2.2"/>
  <text x="728" y="265" font-size="14" fill="#1d4ed8" font-style="italic" font-weight="bold">col(X)</text>
  <circle cx="100" cy="260" r="5" fill="#1e293b"/>
  <line x1="100" y1="260" x2="488" y2="68" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="9,3" marker-end="url(#arrT2)"/>
  <text x="496" y="63" font-size="15" font-weight="bold" fill="#dc2626">T</text>
  <line x1="492" y1="250" x2="492" y2="78" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#arrTtilde2)"/>
  <text x="505" y="170" font-size="16" font-weight="bold" fill="#15803d">T̃</text>
  <polyline points="474,260 474,242 492,242" fill="none" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <line x1="100" y1="260" x2="278" y2="78" stroke="#d97706" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="9,3" marker-end="url(#arrY)"/>
  <text x="262" y="68" font-size="15" font-weight="bold" fill="#d97706">Y</text>
  <line x1="282" y1="250" x2="282" y2="88" stroke="#92400e" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#arrYtilde)"/>
  <text x="245" y="172" font-size="16" font-weight="bold" fill="#92400e">Ỹ</text>
  <polyline points="264,260 264,242 282,242" fill="none" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <line x1="282" y1="150" x2="492" y2="145" stroke="#1a2744" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <text x="348" y="138" font-size="14" fill="#1a2744" font-weight="bold">β̂ = slope of Ỹ on T̃</text>
</svg>
```


---

We have not written a single formula yet — and we already know what $\hat\beta$ is.

$\hat\beta$ is the slope between $\tilde Y$ and $\tilde T$: both stripped of everything $X$ can linearly explain.

<br>

**"Controlling for $X$" means working in the space orthogonal to $X$.**

The formula is just the algebraic statement of the picture we just drew.

::: {.callout-note}
Every coefficient in every multivariate OLS you have ever run is already a partialling-out estimator. FWL proves it — and in doing so tells you exactly when the approach works and exactly when it fails.
:::


# Part 2 · The Theorem {background-color="#1a2744"}

*Formalise what the geometry already showed*


---

## The Frisch-Waugh-Lovell Theorem {.smaller}

**Theorem (FWL, 1933–1963)**

The OLS coefficient $\hat\beta$ on $T$ in the full regression $Y = \alpha + \beta T + \gamma' X + \varepsilon$ is *numerically identical* to the coefficient from:

| Step | Operation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Regress $T$ on $X$ → save residuals | $\tilde T$ — variation in T unexplained by X |
| **2** | Regress $Y$ on $X$ → save residuals | $\tilde Y$ — variation in Y unexplained by X |
| **3** | Regress $\tilde Y$ on $\tilde T$ (no intercept) | $\hat\beta$ — *identical to the full regression* |

Steps 1 and 2 can be done in either order.


---

::: {.callout-warning}
**Both sides must be residualised.**

Regressing raw $Y$ on $\tilde T$ — skipping Step 2 — gives a *different* estimator.

Raw $Y$ still contains $\gamma' X$, the direct influence of quality on sales. Since quality correlates with $\tilde T$ through the unpartialled outcome side, $\hat\beta$ picks up part of quality's effect and inflates above the true value.

Nothing in the regression output flags this mistake. This is the most common FWL implementation error. The diagnostic: if your FWL coefficient disagrees with the full OLS, check whether you residualised both sides.
:::


---

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
T_s = pd.Series(T, name="T");  W_s = pd.Series(W, name="W")

# Step 1 — residualise T on W
T_tilde = sm.OLS(T_s, sm.add_constant(W_s)).fit().resid

# Step 2 — residualise Y on W
Y_tilde = sm.OLS(Y, sm.add_constant(W_s)).fit().resid

# Step 3 — regress Ỹ on T̃
beta_fwl  = sm.OLS(Y_tilde, T_tilde).fit().params.iloc[0]
beta_full = sm.OLS(Y, sm.add_constant(data[["T", "W"]])).fit().params["T"]

print(f"Full OLS β̂  = {beta_full:.10f}")
print(f"FWL Step 3  = {beta_fwl:.10f}")
print(f"Difference  = {abs(beta_full - beta_fwl):.2e}")
```

*Post-lecture notebook: reproduce all three steps, then plot $\tilde Y$ against $\tilde T$.*


---

## Proof sketch — the annihilator matrix {.smaller}

> *Optional — follow the algebra or trust the picture*

Define $M_X = I - X(X'X)^{-1}X'$. Three properties:

| Property | Statement | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Idempotent** | $M_X^2 = M_X$ | Projecting twice = projecting once |
| **Annihilates $X$** | $M_X X = 0$ | Any vector in col($X$) maps to zero |
| **Projects to col$(X)^\perp$** | $M_X v \perp X$ for any $v$ | Output is always orthogonal to $X$ |

Note $M_X T = \tilde T$ and $M_X Y = \tilde Y$ — precisely the FWL residuals. Therefore:

$$\hat\beta_{\text{OLS}} = (T' M_X T)^{-1} T' M_X Y = (\tilde T' \tilde T)^{-1} \tilde T' \tilde Y = \hat\beta_{\text{FWL}} \qquad \square$$


---

## ✎ Checkpoint 1 — the question

**Part A — explain**

In words: why does Step 3 of FWL give *exactly* the same coefficient as full OLS? What is it about $\tilde T$ that makes the equivalence hold?

<br>

**Part B — diagnose**

A colleague runs the three FWL steps and gets $\hat\beta = 1.7$.
The full OLS gives $\hat\beta = 1.5$.
Assuming no arithmetic errors — what went wrong?

. . .

*Hint: what is $\tilde T$ orthogonal to? What does that mean for adding $X$ to Step 3?*


---

## Checkpoint 1 — Part A {.smaller}

**Why does Step 3 give exactly the same number as the full regression?**

$\tilde T$ is orthogonal to col($X$) by construction — that is literally what residualising does.

Adding $X$ as a regressor in Step 3 would change nothing: $X$ has no additional explanatory power for anything already orthogonal to it.

The full regression and FWL Step 3 are solving the same optimisation in the same space. Exact equality follows — not approximately, to machine precision.

::: {.callout-note}
The geometry says the same thing: $\tilde T$ lives in the orthogonal complement of col($X$). Regressing in that space is equivalent to the full regression restricted to that space.
:::


---

## Checkpoint 1 — Part B {.smaller}

**What procedural error produces $\hat\beta = 1.7$ instead of $1.5$?**

The colleague residualised $T$ (Step 1) correctly, but regressed **raw $Y$** on $\tilde T$ instead of $\tilde Y$ on $\tilde T$.

Raw $Y$ still contains $\gamma' X$ — quality's direct effect on sales. Since quality correlates with $\tilde T$ through the unpartialled outcome side, $\hat\beta$ picks up part of quality's effect on sales and inflates above the true 1.5.

<br>

**Rule: both variables must be residualised. Skipping either side breaks the equivalence.**

::: {.callout-note}
**Exam signal:** diagnosing a FWL / full OLS mismatch is a fair exam question. The answer always traces back to one of the two residuals being wrong or missing.
:::


# Part 3 · When It Breaks {background-color="#1a2744"}

*FWL is exact — but only if you partial out the right things, correctly*


---

## Failure mode 1 — the collider trap

FWL tells you *how* to partial out. Not *what* to partial out. That is a DAG question.

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 720 255" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:96%;max-width:720px;margin:auto;display:block">
  <defs>
    <marker id="aC" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="7" refX="8" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 9 3.5, 0 7" fill="#dc2626"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="aG2" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="7" refX="8" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 9 3.5, 0 7" fill="#16a34a"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="aB" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="7" refX="8" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 9 3.5, 0 7" fill="#2563eb"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <line x1="360" y1="0" x2="360" y2="255" stroke="#e2e8f0" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5,3"/>
  <text x="180" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e293b">Confounder C</text>
  <text x="180" y="38" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#64748b">causes both T and Y</text>
  <circle cx="180" cy="90" r="34" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2.2"/>
  <text x="180" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#991b1b">C</text>
  <circle cx="80" cy="190" r="34" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="80" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#1e293b">T</text>
  <circle cx="280" cy="190" r="34" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="280" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#1e293b">Y</text>
  <line x1="158" y1="118" x2="105" y2="163" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#aC)"/>
  <line x1="202" y1="118" x2="255" y2="163" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#aC)"/>
  <line x1="116" y1="190" x2="242" y2="190" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="2.2" marker-end="url(#aG2)"/>
  <text x="180" y="240" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#16a34a" font-weight="bold">✓ Control for C</text>

  <text x="540" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e293b">Collider L</text>
  <text x="540" y="38" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#64748b">caused by both T and Y</text>
  <circle cx="440" cy="190" r="34" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="440" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#1e293b">T</text>
  <circle cx="640" cy="190" r="34" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="640" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#1e293b">Y</text>
  <circle cx="540" cy="90" r="34" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2.2"/>
  <text x="540" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#1d4ed8">L</text>
  <line x1="462" y1="163" x2="515" y2="118" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#aB)"/>
  <line x1="618" y1="163" x2="565" y2="118" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#aB)"/>
  <line x1="476" y1="190" x2="602" y2="190" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="2.2" marker-end="url(#aG2)"/>
  <text x="540" y="240" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#dc2626" font-weight="bold">✗ Do NOT control for L</text>
</svg>
```

**Draw your DAG before running anything.** FWL on the wrong adjustment set gives an exact answer to the wrong question.


---

## Failure mode 2 — non-linear confounders

OLS residualisation removes only the **linear** part of $X$'s influence on $T$.

```{python}
#| label: nonlinear-plot
#| fig-height: 3.2
#| fig-width: 10

rng2 = np.random.default_rng(42)
n_nl = 400
W_nl = rng2.uniform(-2.5, 2.5, n_nl)
T_nl = W_nl**2 + rng2.normal(0, 0.5, n_nl)
Y_nl = 1.5 * T_nl + 2.0 * W_nl**2 + rng2.normal(0, 1, n_nl)
T_tilde_lin = sm.OLS(T_nl, sm.add_constant(W_nl)).fit().resid

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 3.2))
ax = axes[0]
ax.scatter(W_nl, T_nl, alpha=0.25, s=14, color="#dc2626")
x_fit = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 200)
ax.plot(x_fit, np.polyval(np.polyfit(W_nl, T_nl, 1), x_fit),
        color="#6b7280", lw=2.5, linestyle="--", label="OLS linear fit")
ax.plot(x_fit, x_fit**2, color="#2563eb", lw=2.5, label="true: W²")
ax.set_xlabel("W  (confounder)", fontsize=12); ax.set_ylabel("T  (treatment)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title("Step 1 input: T vs W", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(fontsize=10); ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False); ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)

ax = axes[1]
ax.scatter(W_nl, T_tilde_lin, alpha=0.25, s=14, color="#16a34a")
ax.axhline(0, color="#6b7280", lw=1.5, linestyle="--")
ax.plot(x_fit, np.polyval(np.polyfit(W_nl, T_tilde_lin, 2), x_fit),
        color="#dc2626", lw=2.5, label="residual trend")
ax.set_xlabel("W  (confounder)", fontsize=12); ax.set_ylabel("T̃  (linear residual)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title("T̃ after linear partialling: curve still visible", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(fontsize=10); ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False); ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```


---

**T̃ is free of the *linear* effect of X — not free of X.**

That is a strictly weaker guarantee.

If residuals show structure when plotted against a control, partialling is incomplete — and $\hat\beta$ is biased. The regression output gives no warning.

::: {.callout-warning}
This is the gap that Double ML closes. A flexible ML model in Steps 1 & 2 fits the true curve — not just a straight line. The residuals it produces are genuinely orthogonal to $X$, not just linearly orthogonal.
:::


---

## Failure mode 3 — high dimensions {.smaller}

| Regime | What breaks |
|---|---|
| $p > n$ | $(X'X)$ is singular — OLS cannot be computed |
| $p$ close to $n$ | OLS overfits Steps 1 & 2 — residuals are noise |
| Non-linear confounders | Residuals still contain confounding |
| Many correlated controls | $\tilde T$ has near-zero variance → huge SE on $\hat\beta$ |

The right question is not "can I include more controls?" **The right question is: can I partial out $X$ from $T$ and $Y$ correctly at this scale?**

OLS says no beyond a certain point. But the *structure* of FWL still holds — we just need a more flexible tool for the partialling steps. That is what Double ML provides.


---

## ✎ Checkpoint 2 — the question

A public health researcher estimates the effect of air pollution ($T$) on hospital admissions ($Y$), controlling **linearly** for income ($X$).

Income has a strongly **concave** effect on both pollution exposure and health outcomes.

They report $\hat\beta = 0.8$ with a tight confidence interval.

<br>

**Should you trust this? What exactly goes wrong — and is the bias visible in the output?**

. . .

*Hint: what does OLS partialling remove from $T$? What does it leave behind?*


---

## Checkpoint 2 — answer {.smaller}

**No. Linear partialling removes only the linear component of income's influence.**

The concave (non-linear) part remains inside $\tilde T$ — so $\tilde T$ is not truly free of income.

**The bias chain:**

1. OLS fits a straight line through (income, pollution) in Step 1
2. The concave residual stays in $\tilde T$ — high pollution at very low *and* very high incomes
3. That residual correlates with hospital admissions through income's non-linear health effect
4. Step 3 attributes that correlation to pollution

**Is the bias visible in the output?** No. Small standard errors and a significant p-value confirm statistical power, not correct identification. The diagnostic is to plot $\tilde T$ against income: a visible curve means partialling is incomplete.


# Part 4 · Double Machine Learning {background-color="#1a2744"}

*Replace OLS in Steps 1 & 2 — the fix is almost embarrassingly simple*


---

## The simple insight {.smaller}

FWL says: partial out $X$ from $T$ and $Y$, then regress residuals.

**It does not say to use OLS for the partialling.**

<br>

Replace Steps 1 & 2 with any flexible ML learner:

| Step | What we fit | Residual |
|---|---|---|
| **1a** | $\hat g(X) = \mathbb{E}[Y \mid X]$ via ML | $\tilde Y = Y - \hat g(X)$ |
| **1b** | $\hat m(X) = \mathbb{E}[T \mid X]$ via ML | $\tilde T = T - \hat m(X)$ |
| **2** | OLS of $\tilde Y$ on $\tilde T$ | $\hat\theta$ — causal effect |

Any learner works: LASSO, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Neural Net.

The FWL algebra is unchanged. ML does the heavy lifting in Steps 1 & 2.


---

## Why naïve swap fails {.smaller}

Plugging ML directly into Steps 1 & 2 without care introduces two new problems:

**Problem 1 — Regularisation**

ML shrinks predictions toward the mean. Residuals are systematically too large. The signal in $\tilde T$ is distorted.

**Problem 2 — Overfitting contamination**

ML trained and evaluated on the *same* data memorises noise. In-sample residuals are too small — they under-represent true unexplained variation.

<br>

Both problems corrupt $\tilde T$ and $\tilde Y$ before Step 2 runs.

**Cross-fitting fixes both.**


---

## Cross-fitting — the idea {.smaller}

**The problem:** ML memorises training data. In-sample residuals are contaminated.

**The solution:** for each observation, generate its residual from a model that *never saw it*.

<br>

This is K-fold cross-validation — but instead of evaluating prediction accuracy, you are generating **honest residuals** for the causal OLS.

::: {.callout-tip}
Each fold's residuals come from a model trained on all *other* folds. No overfitting contamination. The residuals are honest — as if you had infinite separate datasets.

K = 5 is standard: 80% training data per round, stable across runs.
:::

---

## Cross-fitting — the procedure

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 720 340" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:96%;max-width:720px;margin:auto;display:block">
  <defs>
    <marker id="aDown" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="7" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 8 3, 0 6" fill="#475569"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="aRight2" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="7" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <polygon points="0 0, 8 3, 0 6" fill="#475569"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <text x="16" y="65" font-size="12" fill="#64748b" font-weight="bold">Round 1</text>
  <rect x="55" y="38" width="278" height="56" fill="#dbeafe" rx="6" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="194" y="63" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#1d4ed8">Fold 1 · TRAIN</text>
  <text x="194" y="81" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#3b82f6">fit ML model here</text>
  <rect x="385" y="38" width="278" height="56" fill="#dcfce7" rx="6" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="524" y="61" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#15803d">Fold 2 · PREDICT</text>
  <text x="524" y="79" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#16a34a">→ T̃₂, Ỹ₂</text>
  <line x1="336" y1="66" x2="382" y2="66" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.8" marker-end="url(#aRight2)"/>
  <text x="16" y="162" font-size="12" fill="#64748b" font-weight="bold">Round 2</text>
  <rect x="55" y="135" width="278" height="56" fill="#dcfce7" rx="6" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="194" y="158" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#15803d">Fold 1 · PREDICT</text>
  <text x="194" y="176" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#16a34a">→ T̃₁, Ỹ₁</text>
  <rect x="385" y="135" width="278" height="56" fill="#dbeafe" rx="6" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="524" y="160" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#1d4ed8">Fold 2 · TRAIN</text>
  <text x="524" y="178" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#3b82f6">fit ML model here</text>
  <line x1="382" y1="163" x2="336" y2="163" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.8" marker-end="url(#aRight2)"/>
  <line x1="360" y1="28" x2="360" y2="205" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="5,3"/>
  <rect x="130" y="228" width="460" height="46" rx="6" fill="#f8fafc" stroke="#64748b" stroke-width="1.8"/>
  <text x="360" y="248" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#1e293b">Stack: {T̃₁, Ỹ₁, T̃₂, Ỹ₂}</text>
  <text x="360" y="266" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#64748b">full-sample honest residuals</text>
  <line x1="194" y1="193" x2="278" y2="226" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#aDown)"/>
  <line x1="524" y1="193" x2="442" y2="226" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#aDown)"/>
  <rect x="230" y="302" width="260" height="36" rx="6" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#d97706" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="360" y="325" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#92400e">OLS of Ỹ on T̃ → θ̂</text>
  <line x1="360" y1="276" x2="360" y2="300" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.8" marker-end="url(#aDown)"/>
</svg>
```


---

## DML — the algorithm

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold

kf      = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
T_tilde = np.zeros(n)
Y_tilde = np.zeros(n)

for train_idx, val_idx in kf.split(X):
    for target, store in [(T, T_tilde), (Y, Y_tilde)]:
        rf = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)
        rf.fit(X[train_idx], target[train_idx])
        store[val_idx] = target[val_idx] - rf.predict(X[val_idx])

# Pure FWL on honest residuals
theta_dml = np.dot(T_tilde, Y_tilde) / np.dot(T_tilde, T_tilde)
```

::: {.callout-note}
**Post-lecture notebook:** you implement this from scratch — same DGP, five-fold cross-fitting, and a four-estimator comparison figure reused in the Week 12 deck.
:::


---

## DML — when to use what

| Estimator | Non-linearity | High-$p$ | Valid SE |
|---|---|---|---|
| Naïve OLS (no controls) | ✗ | — | ✗ biased |
| OLS + linear controls | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ if truly linear |
| OLS + FWL (manual) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ exact |
| **Double ML** | **✓** | **✓** | **✓ $\sqrt{n}$-consistent** |

<br>

OLS + FWL is not inferior to DML in low dimensions — it is exact. DML is needed when OLS's linear partialling assumption breaks.

**Use FWL to build intuition. Use DML in practice.**


# Part 5 · Neyman Orthogonality {background-color="#1a2744"}

*Why residualising both sides is structurally necessary*


---

## Why the "double" matters

**The question:** DML residualises *both* $T$ and $Y$. Why not just $T$?

<br>

**Single residualisation** — residualise $T$ only

Errors in $\hat m(X)$ enter $\hat\theta$ **linearly** — proportional to $\delta_m$. If ML converges at rate $n^{-1/4}$, bias shrinks too slowly. Confidence intervals fail at any sample size.

<br>

**Double residualisation** — residualise both

Errors in $\hat m$ and $\hat g$ enter as a **product** $\delta_m \times \delta_g$ — second-order small. If both converge at $n^{-1/4}$, the product is $n^{-1/2}$ — valid inference.


---

## Why the "double" matters — the intuition

::: {.callout-tip}
**The ruler analogy**

Your ruler is off by 1mm. Your scale is off by 1g.

Combined error ≈ 1mm × 1g ≈ 0. Not 2mm, not 2g.

Two small errors **multiplied** are negligible. One is not.

DML exploits this multiplicative structure — by residualising both sides, errors from the two ML models cancel each other at second order.
:::


---

## Neyman orthogonality — formal {.smaller}

> *Skippable — included for exam coverage*

The DML score function for the partially linear model:

$$\psi(\theta;\, \hat g, \hat m) = \bigl(Y - \theta D - \hat g(X)\bigr) \cdot \tilde T$$

**Neyman orthogonality** means the score's derivative with respect to the nuisance functions vanishes at the truth:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial g} \,\mathbb{E}[\psi(\theta_0;\, g, m_0)] \bigg|_{g = g_0} = 0$$

Small perturbations in $\hat g$ and $\hat m$ produce zero first-order effect on $\hat\theta$. ML errors enter at second order only — hence the $\delta_m \times \delta_g$ product structure.

Residualising only $T$ gives a score where $\partial\psi/\partial g \neq 0$ — error enters linearly. Residualising $Y$ achieves orthogonality.

*Exam signal: you will not be asked to derive the score. You may be asked why single residualisation fails.*


---

## ✎ Checkpoint 3 — the question

A colleague implements DML:

- Step 1: fit $\hat m(X) = \mathbb{E}[T \mid X]$ via Random Forest → $\tilde T = T - \hat m(X)$
- Step 2: regress **raw $Y$** (not residualised) on $\tilde T$

They argue this saves computation and "$Y$ doesn't need cleaning."

<br>

**Are they right? What bias does this introduce — and does it vanish as $n \to \infty$?**

. . .

*Hint: what does raw $Y$ still contain? How do errors in $\hat m$ enter the estimate — linearly or as a product?*


---

## Checkpoint 3 — answer {.smaller}

**The colleague is wrong.** Raw $Y$ still contains $g(X)$ — $X$'s influence on the outcome.

The score becomes $g(X) \cdot \tilde T + \varepsilon \cdot \tilde T$. The second term vanishes. The first does not — unless $\hat m$ is perfect, which it never is.

| Setup | Bias size | Vanishes at $\sqrt{n}$ rate? |
|---|---|---|
| Single residualisation | $O(\delta_m)$ — linear in ML error | **No** |
| Double residualisation | $O(\delta_m \times \delta_g)$ | **Yes** |

If ML converges at $n^{-1/4}$: single → bias $\sim n^{-1/4}$ (too slow), double → $\sim n^{-1/2}$ (valid).

This is not an asymptotic refinement. It is a structural flaw that persists at every sample size — the colleague's shortcut permanently invalidates inference.


---

## What we covered

1. **Geometry** — "controlling for $X$" means working in the space orthogonal to col($X$)

2. **FWL theorem** — every OLS coefficient is a partialling-out estimator, exactly, to machine precision

3. **Three failure modes** — collider bias, non-linear confounders, high dimensions

4. **Double ML** — replace OLS partialling with ML + cross-fitting; the FWL algebra is unchanged

5. **Neyman orthogonality** — residualise both sides so errors multiply, not add


---

## Week 12 preview

$$\underbrace{\text{FWL}}_{\text{today}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{DML}}_{\text{Week 12}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{CATE}}_{\text{Week 15}}$$

**Next week:** implement DML end-to-end using `doubleml` and `econml` — production implementations with valid standard errors and confidence intervals.

We connect to the **R-learner** for heterogeneous treatment effects: not just the average effect, but *who benefits and by how much*.

::: {.callout-note}
**Post-lecture notebook is live.** It builds directly on today — all three FWL steps in code, then breaking OLS partialling on purpose, then a from-scratch DML implementation. The figures you produce are reused in next week's deck.
:::

---

## Appendix — proof of $M_X$ properties {.smaller}

$M_X = I - X(X'X)^{-1}X'$

**Idempotent:** $M_X^2 = M_X - M_X X(X'X)^{-1}X' = M_X - 0 = M_X$

**Annihilates $X$:** $M_X X = X - X(X'X)^{-1}X'X = X - X = 0$

**Symmetric:** $(M_X)' = I - X\bigl((X'X)^{-1}\bigr)'X' = M_X$ since $(X'X)^{-1}$ is symmetric

**Therefore:**

$$\hat\beta_\text{OLS} = (T' M_X T)^{-1} T' M_X Y = (\tilde T' \tilde T)^{-1} \tilde T' \tilde Y = \hat\beta_\text{FWL} \qquad \square$$
